evidence_quotes.quote: The European Commission itself writes that consumers are currently exposed to harmful, addictive or manipulative designs.
in particular in video games and digital media
I have acquired many Assassins Creed titles for XBOX360 and Xbox One, either completely deactivated or curtailed in their functions.
the arbitrary deactivation or alteration by providers of digital content already paid and purchased
whereby providers can unilaterally modify, disable or delete content after purchase
without adequate warning, without compensation and without the possibility of archiving
The Crew, which was completely deactivated despite existing usage rights for paying customers.
Clarification of the notion of purchase in the digital context can only take place if a permanent right of use is granted, which cannot be revoked unilaterally.
Prohibition of the complete deactivation of digital products, unless there are compelling reasons (e.g. security, data protection).
Obligation to provide alternative means of use (e.g. offline mode, DRM-free export) when services are switched off.
Refund or conversion options in case of post-purchase functional limitations.
Consumer right to archiving and local preservation of purchased digital media.

paragraphs.text: Ladies and gentlemen, as part of the consultation on the planned Digital Fairness Act, as affected consumers and EU citizens, I would like to draw attention to an increasingly critical protection gap: the arbitrary deactivation or alteration by providers of digital content already paid and purchased, in particular in video games and digital media. A key issue is the laundering of the concept of purchase in the digital space. Consumers believe that, by purchasing a digital product, they acquire a permanent right of use similar to a physical book, film or game. In practice, however, they are often time-limited, revocable rights of use, whereby providers can unilaterally modify, disable or delete content after purchase. From the point of view of consumer protection, this contradiction between the concept of purchase and actual control of the product constitutes deception. This is particularly true for platforms such as Amazon Kindle, but also for game epublisers such as Ubisoft. I have acquired many Assassins Creed titles for XBOX360 and Xbox One, either completely deactivated or curtailed in their functions. Among them: Assassins Creed Identity (comprehensible since 2021) Assassins Creed Brotherhood, Revelations, AC III Multiplayer (termination 2024) Assassins Creed IV Black Flag & Unity (Multiplayer at risk) These measures were carried out without adequate warning, without compensation and without the possibility of archiving. Such practices run counter to the principle of property protection, undermine trust in digital markets and affect millions of EU citizens. The focus was particularly on the Ubisoft game The Crew, which was completely deactivated despite existing usage rights for paying customers. Although the Commission’s feedback to date points to possible infringements of Directive 93/13/EEC, there is still no clear legal classification and enforcement. I therefore recommend that the following elements be included in the envisaged act: 1. Clarification of the notion of purchase in the digital context can only take place if a permanent right of use is granted, which cannot be revoked unilaterally. 2. Prohibition of the complete deactivation of digital products, unless there are compelling reasons (e.g. security, data protection). 3. Obligation to provide alternative means of use (e.g. offline mode, DRM-free export) when services are switched off. 4. Refund or conversion options in case of post-purchase functional limitations. 5. Consumer right to archiving and local preservation of purchased digital media. The European Commission itself writes that consumers are currently exposed to harmful, addictive or manipulative designs. I therefore call for this logic to be extended to the area of digital property rights. The deliberate restriction or deletion of paid products after sale is an expression of structural unfairness and undermines fundamental rights, in particular in the area of cultural heritage and digital sustainability. Thank you for the opportunity to comment. I strongly support the objectives of this initiative and I hope for strong regulation that delivers clarity, trust and fairness to the Digital Single Market.
